item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really thought this was gonna be a herman Fuller SCP but it works really well even without that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enjoyable scip with a nasty twist at the end. I also liked the inclusion of the fallibility of Foundation researchers. That said I would love to read the new (presumably draconian) containment procedures given that 1293s can seemingly engineer their offspring to whatever is required. I'm imagining a containment breach caused by a single 1293 which deliberately delayed reproduction in case of emergencies and produces a perfect replica of one of the researchers or security personnel. +1 for the scip and for the inspiration for the disturbing tale I just thought of!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Greetings, loin mother! I am child!

I think this is the first time I've actually laughed out loud from something on this site!
Read the 1293-A dialogue in Ned Flanders' (Simpsons) voice, now I can't get the image of Flanders in a mascot costume vomiting out another, smaller Flanders. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I agree, there's devotion to a cause and your job to preserve humanity, but sacrificing your own kids? That's pretty unbelievable

item.title: Image Update
item.content:encoded: This article contained copyrighted images adjacent to hand-drawn representations. Copyright holder specifically forbade use without authorized consent. I have attempted contact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno why, but the way the loin child talked reminded me of Jimbles Notronbo…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I stumbled onto this when I googled Creepy Childerns drawings….
for my own scp fuel..
I dont regret the nightmares I got from the pictures..
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done, +1. The interview log is easily one of the funniest I've read on the wiki.

However, 2 things:

1. I can't see the Foundation allowing any children to come into contact with 1293, much less children of personnel.

2. This article is in dire need of a recovery log, just enough to give us a little bit of context. Without it, this feels a little too close to a -j for comfort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a wibbly-wobbly-wonderfull article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(SCP-1293-A-4 disregards Dr. Reeves and begins dancing.)

Squeedle-deedle-diddly-dee!
Please take this +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's been a while. I think we're all wondering how the squeedles are taking their new accommodations.

Also,

Squeedle-Deedle-Doo!
+1 for you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Autopsies performed on deceased specimens have shown that SCP-1293 are completely unclothed, with skin, bone, and muscle tissue mimicking various types of fabric.

This material is actually a part of SCP-049’s body, as microscopic and genetic testing show it to be similar in structure to muscle, although it feels much like rough leather, and the mask much like ceramic.

Is 049 actually an instance of 1293?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for you memetic effect break me mind. Now can't find reason not to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They were playing the Foundation for fools, for one thing. For another, they were forcing the Foundation to increase their numbers. If population controls weren't in place (and I don't think they were), these things were just casually reproducing and feeding off the Foundation while the Foundation was losing people to the -Cs and/or letting it happen.
Although, I do admit it's not as impressive on a second read-through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I seriously don't get it. The big letter at the end seems to be implying some big paradigm-shifting information, but all I got from it was that they mate, and they feed on emotions of some sort for some reason, but are otherwise exactly as described.

item.title: Re: confused
item.content:encoded: You know what? I'm really digging this post. Like, the author is encouraging death of the author. You're putting your readers' imaginations before your own intentions, which allows for some really awesome interpretations. I applaud this practice. Squeedle-Dee Doo, sir/ma'am.

item.title: Re: confused
item.content:encoded:
To preserve the ambiguity of the article, I'll provide several different answers to each question. Feel free to choose whichever answers you like best.

+ A PeppersGhost Choose-Your-Own-Answer Adventure!
- A PeppersGhost Choose-Your-Own-Answer Adventure!

If they breed together, why does A's offspring look like the kid's drawing?

  1. SCP-1293-A instances still require the creative influence of a human child in order to properly give birth.
  2. SCP-1293-A instances have the ability to create whatever sort of offspring they want, but they deliberately design their progeny to look like the kids' drawings in order to maintain their deception.
  3. SCP-1293 are a race of incorporeal, telepathic lifeforms with no inherent physical form. However, they are capable of creating physical shells in order to facilitate their psychological feeding. Human children are used to imprint visual identities onto them because they know that adults will find such forms unpleasant.

What's the deal with the tongue thing?

  1. Dr. Goodwin was simply jumping to conclusions, and the tendril thing is simply how they show affection for one another.
  2. Dr. Goodwin was simply jumping to conclusions, and the tendril thing is just a way of generating unease and bewilderment simultaneously, feeding both types of creatures at once.
  3. Dr. Goodwin was correct in his assumptions, and the tendril thing is a necessary part of the procreation process.

Why does Dr. Goodwin think that SCP-1293-A instances feed off unease?

  1. Dr. Goodwin was desperate to come up with an explanation for their behavior. The theory described in his letter isn't anything more than an educated guess.
  2. Dr. Goodwin has worked for the Foundation for many years and overseen the research of many different SCPs. A good number of the SCPs he's worked with involved some form of connection with human psyche, and this has influenced the way he approaches new projects.
  3. In the SCP universe, it's very common to encounter a monster or entity that feeds off human fear. Knowing this, Dr. Goodwin assumes that SCP-1293-A simply subsist on a less potent version of fear, but will use SCP-1293-C to generate pure terror if they're especially hungry.

To wrap things up, the tongue thing should have been mentioned in the description (since no matter what A says, someone would have noticed it), and I feel that Goodwin is jumping to conclusions, which is just as bad as taking an SCP's word for it.

I'm just going to respond to these points directly.

The tongue thing is a new discovery by Dr. Goodwin. It should be mentioned in the main body of the article, and it will be added in later on (in-universe, not here on the wiki). That's why it says "revisions to [this document] are underway" underneath Goodwin's note.

Yes, Goodwin is making some pretty huge assumptions about these creatures. But is he right?

  1. Goodwin's theory is wrong, and the creatures were telling the truth about themselves the whole time.
  2. Goodwin's theory is correct, and the creatures were just lying about their nature. The Squeedles' weird personalities are just a performance to generate the human emotions they feed on.
  3. Goodwin's theory is mostly correct, AND the creatures were mostly telling the truth. The doctor is correct that they feed off psychological energy, but the Squeedles aren't "faking it" like he implied they are. Since they feed on unease, Squeedles with genuinely unsettling personalities are favored by natural selection. Eating the drawings and kissing with tendrils are both necessary components in the procreation process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thoroughly bizzare, unsettling, entertaining, and a bit scary. Never quite seen that balance anywhere else. Magnificent.

item.title: Re: confused
item.content:encoded:
your explanation doesn't help me that much for some reason. this might be why.

according to the note at the end, he has video evidence of the creatures doing the action, so the fact that it's not mentioned in the description, especially if it's validity isn't certain, implies 2 things to me: that Goodwin "lost" the footage(or he never had it and just wanted a reward for "discovering" something new about an SCP) and/or the foundation wasn't monitoring them that closely before Goodwin was on the scene(unlikely, since the foundation has no sense of personal space towards anomalous things without safety reasons).

at any rate, I was hoping for the author of the article (who, if I'm not mistaking, is PeppersGhost) to give an answer, since if they wrote the thing they'll probably have an easier time explaining it than anyone else.

thanks anyways though

item.title: Re: confused
item.content:encoded:
Well, keep in mind that the note at the end is purely speculation on the doctor's part. The reason why none of the info therein is stated in the Description is because the Foundation isn't sure of its validity.
I imagine if the doctor was correct in his conclusions, the offspring would resemble the drawings to perpetuate the belief that the procedure is how they reproduce so they can continue to feed off the unease.

item.title: confused
item.content:encoded:
to who ever wrote the article
help me understand please

I'm a bit confused about the note at the end.

First off, even though I agree that A and B should be kept in separate chambers from each other, if they breed together, why does A's offspring look like the kid's drawing?

Also, the tongue… even if you take A's word for fact, there is no reason to leave out anything involving their behavior, and just because they tongue each other doesn't mean they're mating. It just mean they like each other very much. Or maybe Bs feed As with the extra weirdness (in which case separating them would lead to the As dying off and Bs getting over fed).

Third, although it makes a small bit of sense to assume that B feeds off of bewilderment (in which case I'm confused enough to open a restaurant for them) because of the dancing events, there's no reason to believe that A feeds off of unease.

To wrap things up, the tongue thing should have been mentioned in the description (since no matter what A says, someone would have noticed it), and I feel that Goodwin is jumping to conclusions, which is just as bad as taking an SCP's word for it.

Over all, this isn't enough to make me downvote, and the images was enough to make me temped to upvote from horror. reading through the article didn't change my mind, so +1 to you sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creeped out my tabletop gaming group just by describing this one to them.
And then one of them nearly passed out laughing when he read the actual article.
+1 because I can't vote any higher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very amusing. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ghost, you are a conoisseur of writing. This is amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would have guessed that he was observed trying to get them to keep breeding. He loved them and didn't set up adequate procedures or even try to prevent them from reproducing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It got my +1. The interview was very weird and I wasnt that into the article until I read the last addendum. Now I just want to know what happens next!
Congrats PeppersGhost!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just don't have words for how much I love this article. No words. So much. Ugh. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god why can't I upvote this several times? I want to upvote it for the interview (which was just so creepily bizarre), and then again for the addendum (which revealed that the bastard was being creepily bizarre on purpose! AAAAAAAAAA)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I just thought this was one of those "make you go wtf" scps, and I was going to no-vote.

Then i figured out from the addendum that these things actually feed off of your reaction of "wtf".

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, am I the only one who imagines their voices as Espa Roba from Yu-Gi-Oh abridged?

item.title: Sack monsters
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, reading that interview really made me feel uneasy. o_o Those things would be terrifying to be around, just because they seem so weird and unpredictable! The child thing really is strange, even though it seems harmless enough, but still. Man, reading through this whole thing, I think it got better and better as it built upon itself. The end note brings up a good point, about how not everything you're told about a subject may be truth. These things are scary yet don't do anything but act weird and give birth to a killing machine if not presented with a child to draw imagination from.

Nice and creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PeppersGhost I will be keeping an eye on you. Between this and the puppy death machine, you're quickly becoming my new favorite writer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah. One of the goodies that made me join- Take my upvote!

Damn if I didn't laugh so hard people at my desk thought I was choking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PFFFF- I choked, made people look at me funny from my desk. …damn youuu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bizarre, slightly disturbing, but good all the same. Upvote. But only because I am not entirely sure what else to do… Aside from run away screaming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this was worryingly good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I learned the word "Terpsichore" from the musical Cats.
"We're quiet enough in the morning hours, we're quiet enough in the afternoon
Reserving our terpsichorian powers to dance by the light of the Jellicle moon!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost there. It's almost interesting, but the best you got to was odd. You started with the boring part, added the hint at the end, and then just dropped it. I don't think you understand the difference between a hook and just ending the article too early and calling it a hook. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The third box made me laugh. Nice.
I kind of want it as a collapsible footnote #5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's something of a combination of both.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To Pig_catapult
:D !

Do not apologize. This was awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In common with other commenters I was planning to downvote until the third addendum. Recasting the Where The Wild Things Are monsters as Red Dwarf Polymorphs who feed off emotions makes a lot of weirdness more explicable, and I'm a sucker for evolving documents where your perception of the skip changes as you proceed. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to imagine, if asked why that one was a girl, the good doctor would boggle at the asker for a moment, then go "She has a dress on, duh."

Also I'd love to see more, maybe under a collapsible if you want to keep the same structure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had meant to respond to this earlier. Sorry about that!

I think that if a more competent researcher had been in charge, they would indeed have made a distinction between the two types of SCP-1293 without immediately labeling them as male and female. However, Dr. Reeves had a problem with taking skips at their word, and when the -As started referring to themselves as "lady-types", that was enough for him to designate them as female.

I'm glad you like the pics! I'd actually been planning on using more than just two, but I was concerned that overdoing the images would hurt the visual structure of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Presumably to continue the deception?
Or maybe 1293 isn't very imaginative?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, except I liked the log at the end. After all, asides from the mating (or what), the researcher who has written it is cooking from water - there's no evidence either of them subsist on emotions, or any reason to think they should (or that it's at all possible)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not polite to ask a lady-type about her special monthly times, mister nosy posy!

That "Oh God O_O" moment, right there.

And then one more addendum. It was… good, but I don't know, I think monstrous menstruation was doing fine for me. Also, it throws a wrench in the works when you think about why the new creatures end up looking just like what was drawn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't believe I'm about to do this, but I disagree with that tag call.

or has anomalous biological reproductive methods…

They mate biologically, then gestate extra-dimensionally (anomaly one), with the option to bio-engineer the offspring to match a random childs drawings (anomaly two), and the young are born full-grown with full adult knowledge (anomaly three).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dagnabbit I looked at this article at around noon and I am still laughing about it two hours later… in a library while trying to catalog periodicals… Hopefully nobody thinks I am going crazy haha! But seriously the execution is excellent although it's still not all that clear to me whether 1293-A and 1293-B are somehow related other than being of the same species and feeding in a similar way. But that interview log is so great and I'm glad you found a fitting use for that image :> I am proud to call you an IRL friend.

(also I will totally draw fanart of this just you wait)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: AAAAAAAAAAAAA

item.title: Re: +100!
item.content:encoded: At +104 at the moment of watching this. Congratulations bro. Probably this will scalate to 200 o/!

item.title: Re: +100!
item.content:encoded:
+ Squeedle deedle dee!
- Squeedle deedle dee!

Thanks, guys. Ya'll are awesome. <3

item.title: Re: +100!
item.content:encoded: Fuck yeah!

item.title: Re: +100!
item.content:encoded: I imagine Pepper is squeedling in delight right now

item.title: Re: +100!
item.content:encoded: I totally want to shake his hand right this very moment.
(Well, it's at +102 right now) Congratulations, Peppersghost! Very enjoyable and definitely impressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I totally get that the B's are shaking things up. The tone of it simply gave me that impression on first read and the well justified reasoning doesn't entirely remove the feeling of author superiority for me..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't so much the author trying to troll the reader as the -B's trying to troll the Foundation. They didn't stop because they were ashamed. Simply doing a dance every month isn't enough to confuse Foundation scientists. They have to mix it up with erratic behavioral patterns and general unpredictability. How did these things learn a dance that's only existed a few months? Why didn't they perform the whole thing? Why did they run off before they were finished? The -B's wanted the Foundation to wonder these things. It's the same reason why they performed four acts of a ballet simultaneously and did the worm for five hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm one who upvoted because of Dr. Goodwin's addendum.

My only issue is with the single ladies event. Not the usage of it but rather the context you put this in (not performing the whole song and 'dispersing quickly in different directions') came off as author trolling the reader and took me out of the article.

I just don't feel that SCP-1293-B being comically ashamed of their own attempts at emotional manipulation and feeding relates to a serious article, though I can see the need for some type of twist at that point in the article.
Maybe if you succeed as much as this article has you're allowed that sort of thing?
